metadata.title: The Optimal Cache Rule That Requires Seeing the Future
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = optimal eviction / future knowledge; CRIMSON = suboptimal eviction / cache miss
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no exchange-argument proof, no LRU formal analysis, no multi-level cache hierarchy. Wonder register.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: A computer's cache holds four pages. Requests keep arriving. Whatever you evict always seems to be the next thing needed. The optimal rule is: always evict the page you won't need for the longest time.

card.copy: The Optimal Cache Rule That Requires Seeing the Future

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A computer's cache holds four pages. Requests keep arriving. Whatever you evict always seems to be the next thing needed. The optimal rule is: always evict the

beats.narration_text: Cache holding a, b, c, d — and incoming request stream: c, d, e, f, a, b. When e arrives, the cache is full. You must evict something. What's the best choice?

production_viz.label: Cache shelf [a,b,c,d]; request stream c,d,e,f,a,b shown as timeline; e arriving needs space

beats.narration_text: Evict a: the next request after e is f — miss — then a — miss, you just evicted it — then b — miss. Four total misses. Evict c instead: c is not needed again in this stream. After e and f — 2 misses — a and b are still in cache. Two total misses.

card.copy: Is there a provably optimal eviction rule?
card.sub: Bélády 1966: always evict the page needed furthest in the future

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Evict a: the next request after e is f — miss — then a — miss, you just evicted it — then b — miss. Four total misses. Evict c instead: c is not needed again in

beats.narration_text: The difference is knowing the future. If you know when each cached page will next be needed, always evict the one with the farthest next use. Bélády's 1966 theorem: no eviction policy achieves fewer misses than this on any input stream.

production_viz.label: Cache items with arc lines to their next request positions; longest arc labeled 'evict'; TEAL=optimal, CRIMSON=suboptimal

beats.narration_text: The intuition: if you evict a page that will be needed soon, you'll pay a miss shortly. Evicting a page needed far in the future delays the pain and maximizes the chance that other useful pages stay in cache.

production_viz.label: Timeline: evict soon-needed page → imminent miss (CRIMSON); evict far-future page → pain delayed (TEAL)

beats.narration_text: Bélády's algorithm is not implementable in real systems — you'd need to know the future request sequence in advance. But it gives us a lower bound: no online algorithm can do better on any sequence. It is the optimal offline algorithm.

production_viz.label: Offline (knows future) vs Online (no future); Bélády = lower bound; TEAL=optimal offline, CRIMSON=online gap

beats.narration_text: The real-world approximation is LRU — least recently used. The intuition: if you haven't used a page recently, you probably won't need it soon. Past usage is a proxy for future usage. LRU is competitive against Bélády on real workloads.

production_viz.label: LRU diagram: past usage as proxy for future; cache slots with recency timestamps; TEAL for LRU rule

beats.narration_text: Cache hit rate matters enormously for performance. A cache that hits 99% of requests versus one that hits 90% is 10 times different in miss frequency. Eviction policy can swing the hit rate by several percentage points on real applications.

production_viz.label: 99% vs 90% hit rate: 1% vs 10% miss rate = 10x miss frequency; bar comparison; CRIMSON=misses, TEAL=hits

beats.narration_text: The exchange argument for Bélády's optimality: suppose an algorithm makes a different eviction choice at some step. We can show by case analysis that swapping to Bélády's choice cannot increase total misses — only maintain or reduce them. Inductively, Bélády's choices are always at least as good.

production_viz.label: Two policies diverge at one choice; swap to Bélády: misses don't increase; induction arrow; TEAL=optimal path

beats.narration_text: Always evict the page needed furthest in the future — provably optimal, requires knowing the future. In practice: LRU approximates it using the past. Two misses instead of four. The policy is the difference.

card.copy: Evict the page needed furthest away
card.sub: Bélády: optimal offline — LRU: practical approximation

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Always evict the page needed furthest in the future — provably optimal, requires knowing the future. In practice: LRU approximates it using the past. Two misses

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
